volumeTitle: The Woman of Andros. The Self-Tormentor. The Eunuch
label: The Self-Tormentor (Section front.cast - Section 1.1.158)

sections.label: Section front.cast
sections.original:
Fabulae Interlocutores .

ANTIPHILA , amica Cliniae . BACCHIS , amica Clitiphonis . CHREMES , Clitiphonis pater . CLINIA , Menedemi filius . CLITIPHO , Chremetis filius . DROMO , servus Menedemi . MENEDEMUS , Cliniae pater . NUTRIX . PHRYGIA , ancilla . SOSTRATA , Chremetis uxor . SYRUS , servus Chremetis .
sections.translation: Dramatis Personae CHREMES , [*] an old gentleman , living in the country . MENEDEMUS , [*] an old gentleman , his neighbor . CLINIA , [*] son of Menedemus . CLITIPHO , [*] son of Chremes . DROMO , [*] son of Clinia . SYRUS , [*] servant of Clitipho . SOSTRATA , [*] wife of Chremes . ANTIPHILA , [*] a young woman beloved by Clinia . BACCHIS . [*] a Courtesan , the mistress of Clitipho . The Nurse of Antiphila . PHRYGIA , [*] maid-servant to Bacchis .

sections.label: Section front.production
sections.original: HEAUTONTIMORUMENOS . ACTA LUDIS MEGALENSIBUS , L . CORNELIO LENTULO L . VALERIO FLACCO AEDILIBUS CURULIBUS . EGERUNT L . AMBIVIUS TURPIO ET L . ATTILIUS PRAENESTINUS . MODOS FECIT FLACCUS CLAUDII . GRAECA EST MENANDRU . ACTA PRIMUM TIBIIS IMPARIBUS ; DEINDE DUABUS DEXTRIS . ACTA III . EDITA M . IUVENTIO ET T . SEMPRONIO COSS .
sections.translation:
THE TITLE OF THE PLAY .
IT is from the Greek of Menander . Performed at the Megalensian Games ; Lucius Cornelius Lentulus and Lucius Valerius Flaccus being Curule Aediles . Ambivius Turpio performed it . Flaccus , the freedman of Claudius , composed the music . The first time it was performed to the music of treble and bass flutes ; the second time , of two treble flutes . It was acted three times ; Marcus Juventius and Titus Sempronius being Consuls . [*]

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.1
sections.original: NE cui sit vestrum mirum , cur partes seni
sections.translation: LEST it should be a matter of surprise to any one of you , why the Poet has assigned to an old man [*] a part that belongs to the young , that I will first explain to you ; [*] and then , the reason for my coming I will disclose . An entire Play from an entire Greek one , [*]

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.5
sections.original: Hodie sum acturus Heautontimorumenon ;
sections.translation: the Heautontimorumenos , I am to-day about to represent , which from a two-fold plot [*] has been made but one . I have shown that it is new , and what it is : next I would mention who it was that wrote it , and whose in Greek it is , if I did not think that the greater part of you are aware .

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.10
sections.original: Nunc , quamobrem has partes didicerim , paucis dabo .
sections.translation: Now , for what reason I have learned this part , in a few words I will explain . The Poet intended me to be a Pleader , [*] not the Speaker of a Prologue ; your decision he asks , and has appointed me the advocate ; if this advocate can avail as much by his oral powers as he has excelled in inventing happily ,

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.15
sections.original: Qui orationem hanc scripsit quam dicturus sum .
sections.translation: who composed this speech which I am about to recite . For as to malevolent rumors spreading abroad that he has mixed together many Greek Plays while writing a few Latin ones , he does not deny that this is the case , and that he does not repent of so doing ; and he affirms that he will do so again .

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.20
sections.original: Habet bonorum exemplum , quo exemplo sibi
sections.translation: He has the example of good Poets ; after which example he thinks it is allowable for him to do what they have done . Then , as to a malevolent old Poet [*] saying that he has suddenly applied himself to dramatic pursuits , relying on the genius of his friends , [*] and not his own natural abilities ;

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.25
sections.original: Arbitrium vestrum , vestra existimatio
sections.translation: on that your judgment your your opinion , will prevail . Wherefore I do entreat you all , that the suggestions of our antagonists may not avail more than those of our favorers . Do you be favorable ; grant the means of prospering to those who afford you the means of being spectators of new Plays ;

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.30
sections.original: Sine vitiis ; ne ille pro se dictum existimet ,
sections.translation: those , I mean , without faults : that he may not suppose this said in his behalf who lately made the public give way to a slave as he ran along in the street ; [*] why should he take a madman ’ s part ? About his faults he will say more when he brings out some other new ones , unless he puts an end to his caviling .

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.35
sections.original: Adeste aequo animo ; date potestatem mihi
sections.translation: Attend with favorable feelings ; grant me the opportunity that I may be allowed to act a quiet Play [*] in silence ; that the servant everlastingly running about , the angry old man , the gluttonous parasite , the impudent sharper , and the greedy procurer , may not have always to be performed by me

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.40
sections.original: Clamore summo , cum labore maximo .
sections.translation: with the utmost expense of voice , and the greatest exertion . For my sake come to the conclusion that this request is fair , that so some portion of my labor may be abridged . For nowadays , those who write new Plays do not spare an aged man . If there is any piece requiring exertion , they come running to me ;

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.45
sections.original: Sin lenis est , ad alium defertur gregem .
sections.translation: but if it is a light one , it is taken to another Company . In the present one the style is pure . Do you make proof , what , in each character , [*] my ability can effect . If I have never greedily set a high price upon my skill , and have come to the conclusion that this is my greatest gain ,

sections.label: Section 1.prologue.50
sections.original: Quam maxime servire vestris commodis ;
sections.translation: as far as possible to be subservient to your convenience , establish in me a precedent , that the young may be anxious rather to please you than themselves .

sections.label: Section 1.1.53
sections.original: Quanquam haec inter nos nupera notitia admodum est ,
sections.translation: Although this acquaintanceship between us is of very recent date , from the time in fact of your purchasing an estate here in the neighborhood ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.56
sections.original: Tamen vel virtus tua me , vel vicinitas ,
sections.translation: yet either your good qualities , or our being neighbors ( which I take to be a sort of friendship ), induces me to inform you , frankly and familiarly , that you appear to me to labor beyond your years ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.60
sections.original: Facere , et praeter quam res te adhortatur tua .
sections.translation: and beyond what your affairs require . For , in the name of Gods and men , what would you have ? What can be your aim ? You are , as I conjecture , sixty years of age , or more . No man in these parts has a better or a more valuable estate , no one

sections.label: Section 1.1.65
sections.original: Servos complures : proinde quasi nemo siet ,
sections.translation: more servants ; and yet you discharge their duties just as diligently as if there were none at all . However early in the morning I go out , and however late in the evening I return home , I see you either digging , or plowing , or doing something , in fact , in the fields .

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.original: Nullum remittis tempus , neque te respicis .
sections.translation: You take respite not an instant , and are quite regardless of yourself . I am very sure that this is not done for your amusement . But really I am vexed how little work is done here . [*] If you were to employ the time you spend in laboring yourself , in keeping your servants at work , you would profit much more .

sections.label: Section 1.1.75
sections.original: Chreme , tantumne est ab re tua oti tibi
sections.translation: Have you so much leisure , Chremes , from your own affairs , that you can attend to those of others — those which don ’ t concern you ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.77
sections.original: Homo sum : humani nihil a me alienum puto .
sections.translation: I am a man , [*] and nothing that concerns a man do I deem a matter of indifference to me . Suppose that I wish either to advise you in this matter , or to be informed myself : if what you do is right , that I may do the same ; if it is not , then that I may dissuade you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.80
sections.original: Mihi sic est usus : tibi ut opus facto est , face .
sections.translation: It ’ s requisite for me to do so ; do you as it is necessary for you to do .

sections.label: Section 1.1.81
sections.original: An cuiquam est usus homini se ut cruciet ?
sections.translation: Is it requisite for any person to torment himself ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.81b
sections.original: Mihi .
sections.translation: It is for me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.82
sections.original: Si quid laboris est , nollem : sed quid istuc mali est ,
sections.translation: If you have any affliction , I could wish it otherwise . But prithee , what sorrow is this of yours ? How have you deserved so ill of yourself ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.83b
sections.original: Eheu !
sections.translation: Alas ! alas !

sections.label: Section 1.1.84
sections.original: Ne lacrima : atque istuc quicquid est fac me ut sciam :
sections.translation: Do not weep , but make me acquainted with it , whatever it is .

sections.label: Section 1.1.85
sections.original: Ne retice : ne verere : crede , inquam , mihi ,
sections.translation: Do not be reserved ; fear nothing ; trust me , I tell you . Either by consolation , or by counsel , or by any means , I will aid you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.87
sections.original: Scire hoc vis ?
sections.translation: Do you wish to know this matter ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.87b
sections.original: Hac quidem causa qua dixi tibi .
sections.translation: Yes , and for the reason I mentioned to you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.88
sections.original: Dicetur .
sections.translation: I will tell you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.88b
sections.original: At istos rastros interea tamen
sections.translation: But still , in the mean time , lay down that rake ; don ’ t fatigue yourself .

sections.label: Section 1.1.89b
sections.original: Minime .
sections.translation: By no means .

sections.label: Section 1.1.89c
sections.original: Quam rem agis ?
sections.translation: What can be your object ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.90
sections.original: Sine me vacivum tempus ne quod dem mihi
sections.translation: Do leave me alone , that I may give myself no respite from my labor .

sections.label: Section 1.1.91b
sections.original: Non sinam , inquam .
sections.translation: I will not allow it , I tell you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.91c
sections.original: Ah , non aequum facis .
sections.translation: Ah ! that ’ s not fair .

sections.label: Section 1.1.92
sections.original: Hui , tam graves hos , quaeso ?
sections.translation: Whew ! such a heavy one as this , pray !

sections.label: Section 1.1.92b
sections.original: Sic meritum est meum .
sections.translation: Such are my deserts .

sections.label: Section 1.1.93
sections.original: Nunc loquere .
sections.translation: Now speak .

sections.label: Section 1.1.93b
sections.original: Filium unicum adolescentulum
sections.translation: I have an only son ,— a young man ,— alas ! why did I say — I have ? — rather I should say , I had one , Chremes :—

sections.label: Section 1.1.95
sections.original: Nunc habeam necne incertum est .
sections.translation: whether I have him now , or not , is uncertain .

sections.label: Section 1.1.95b
sections.original: Quid ita istuc ?
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.95c
sections.original: Scies .
sections.translation: You shall know :— There is a poor old woman here , a stranger from Corinth :— her daughter , a young woman , he fell in love with , insomuch that he almost regarded her as his wife ; all this took place unknown to me . When I discovered the matter , I began to reprove him , not with gentleness ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.100
sections.original: Neque ut animum decuit aegrotum adolescentuli ,
sections.translation: nor in the way suited to the love-sick mind of a youth , but with violence , and after the usual method of fathers . I was daily reproaching him ,— Look you , do you expect to be allowed any longer to act thus , myself , your father , being alive ; to be keeping a mistress pretty much as though your wife ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.105
sections.original: Erras , si id credis , et me ignoras , Clinia .
sections.translation: You are mistaken , Clinia , and you don ’ t know me , if you fancy that . I am willing that you should be called my son , just as long as you do what becomes you ; but if you do not do so , I shall find out how it becomes me to act toward you . This arises from nothing , in fact , but too much idleness .

sections.label: Section 1.1.110
sections.original: Ego istuc aetatis non amori operam dabam ,
sections.translation: At your time of life , I did not devote my time to dalliance , but , in consequence of my poverty , departed hence for Asia , and there acquired in arms both riches and military glory . At length the matter came to this ,— the youth , from hearing the same things so often , and with such severity , was overcome .

sections.label: Section 1.1.115
sections.original: Et aetate putavit me et benevolentia
sections.translation: He supposed that I , through age and affection , had more judgment and foresight for him than him-self . He went off to Asia , Chremes , to serve under the king .

sections.label: Section 1.1.118
sections.original: Quid ais ?
sections.translation: What is it you say ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.118b
sections.original: Clam me profectus , menses tres abest .
sections.translation: He departed without my knowledge — and has been gone these three months .

sections.label: Section 1.1.119
sections.original: Ambo accusandi ; etsi illud inceptum tamen
sections.translation: Both are to be blamed — although I still think this step shows an ingenuous and enterprising disposition .

sections.label: Section 1.1.121
sections.original: Ubi comperi ex iis qui ei fuere conscii ,
sections.translation: When I learned this from those who were in the secret , I returned home sad , and with feelings almost over-whelmed and distracted through grief . I sit down ; my servants run to me ; they take off my shoes : [*] then some make all haste to spread the couches , [*]

sections.label: Section 1.1.125
sections.original: Video alios festinare , lectos sternere ,
sections.translation: and to prepare a repast ; each according to his ability did zealously what he could , in order to alleviate my sorrow . When I observed this , I began to reflect thus :— What ! are so many persons anxious for my sake alone , to pleasure myself only ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.130
sections.original: Ancillae tot me vestiant ? sumtus domi
sections.translation: Are so many female servants to provide me with dress ? [*] Shall I alone keep up such an expensive establishment , while my only son , who ought equally , or even more so , to enjoy these things — inasmuch as his age is better suited for the enjoyment of them — him , poor youth , have I driven away from home by my severity !

sections.label: Section 1.1.135
sections.original: Malo quidem me dignum quovis deputem ,
sections.translation: Were I to do this , really I should deem myself deserving of any calamity . But so long as he leads this life of penury , banished from his country through my severity , I will revenge his wrongs upon myself , toiling , making money , saving , and laying up for him .

sections.label: Section 1.1.140
sections.original: Ita facio prorsus : nihil relinquo in aedibus
sections.translation: At once I set about it ; I left nothing in the house , neither movables [*] nor clothing ; every thing I scraped together . Slaves , male and female , except those who could easily pay for their keep by working in the country , all of them I set up to auction and sold . I at once put up a bill

sections.label: Section 1.1.145
sections.original: Aedes mercede ; quasi talenta ad quindecim
sections.translation: to sell my house . [*] I collected somewhere about fifteen talents , and purchased this farm ; here I fatigue myself : I have come to this conclusion , Chremes , that I do my son a less injury , while I am unhappy ; and that it is not right for me to enjoy any pleasure here ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.150
sections.original: Nisi ubi ille huc salvus redierit meus particeps .
sections.translation: until such time as he returns home safe to share it with me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.151
sections.original: Ingenio te esse in liberos leni puto ,
sections.translation: I believe you to be of an affectionate disposition toward your children , [*] and him to be an obedient son , if one were to manage him rightly or prudently . But neither did you understand him sufficiently well , nor he you — a thing that happens where persons don ’ t live on terms of frankness together .

sections.label: Section 1.1.155
sections.original: Tu illum numquam ostendisti quanti penderes ,
sections.translation: You never showed him how highly you valued him , nor did he ever dare put that confidence in you which is due to a father . Had this been done , these troubles would never have befallen you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.158
sections.original: Ita res est , fateor : peccatum a me maximum est .
sections.translation: Such is the fact , I confess ; the greatest fault is on my side .
